sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Electrocardiography (ECG) records the electrical activity of the heart over time. Prehospital 12-lead ECG acquisition is a key intervention for time-sensitive cardiac conditions, particularly STEMI, and has been shown to reduce door-to-balloon times. Continuous 3- or 4-lead monitoring is used for rhythm assessment and arrhythmia detection.

sections.title: Electrode Placement
sections.content:
**Limb Leads (4 electrodes)**
- RA (Right Arm): right wrist or right shoulder/upper arm
- LA (Left Arm): left wrist or left shoulder/upper arm
- RL (Right Leg): right ankle or right lower abdomen / hip
- LL (Left Leg): left ankle or left lower abdomen / hip

**Precordial Leads (6 electrodes) - 12-Lead**
- V1: 4th ICS, right sternal border
- V2: 4th ICS, left sternal border
- V3: Between V2 and V4
- V4: 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line
- V5: Anterior axillary line, horizontal to V4
- V6: Mid-axillary line, horizontal to V4 and V5

**Right-Sided Leads (RV infarct screening)**
- V3R–V6R: Mirror image of left precordial leads on right chest
- Indicated when: inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF elevation), bradycardia, hypotension

**Posterior Leads (posterior STEMI)**
- V7: Posterior axillary line
- V8: Tip of left scapula
- V9: Left paraspinal region
- Indicated when: ST depression in V1–V3 (may represent posterior STEMI)

**MCL1 (Modified Chest Lead 1 - continuous monitoring)**
- Positive electrode: 4th ICS, right sternal border (V1 position)
- Negative electrode: left shoulder / outer left clavicle
- Ground electrode: right shoulder / outer right clavicle
- Use: single-lead continuous rhythm monitoring when a full 12-lead is not in place; resembles V1 and is optimal for P wave identification, bundle branch block morphology, and differentiating supraventricular from ventricular rhythms

sections.title: Acquisition & Troubleshooting
sections.content:
**For quality 12-lead:**
1. Prepare skin: clip chest hair if necessary; dry skin; use abrasive pad if poor contact
2. Apply electrodes to correct anatomical positions
3. Ensure patient is still and warm - shivering causes artefact
4. Enter patient demographics before acquisition if time permits
5. Acquire tracing; assess quality before transmitting or printing
6. Record time of acquisition

**Artefact Sources:**
- Patient movement or shivering: muscle/motion artefact - baseline wander
- Loose electrode or poor contact: wandering baseline, flat line on one lead
- Electrical interference (60 Hz): regular fine oscillations
- Reversed limb leads: inverted P waves and QRS in Lead I; check aVR

**STEMI Criteria (for cath lab activation):**
- ST elevation ≥ 1 mm in ≥ 2 contiguous limb leads
- ST elevation ≥ 2 mm in ≥ 2 contiguous precordial leads (V1–V4)
- New LBBB with clinical presentation consistent with ACS
- Posterior changes: ST depression V1–V3 + tall R waves (posterior STEMI)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- A 12-lead ECG should be acquired and transmitted early in suspected ACS where feasible
- Electrode placement errors are a common cause of diagnostic inaccuracy
- Always print or transmit with patient ID, time, and clinical context
- Serial ECGs are important because the initial tracing may be non-diagnostic in evolving infarction
- Reciprocal changes increase confidence, but the whole clinical picture still matters
- A normal ECG does not rule out serious cardiac pathology in the prehospital phase
